summary: DHH discusses the limitations of 3GPP video format on mobile phones, which caps playback at 15 frames per second and 64 kbit/sec throughput. While acknowledging 3GPP is watchable, he notes it falls far short of unrestricted MPEG-4 quality. He poses the question of whether full MPEG-4 decoding could run directly on a Nokia 3650, suggesting the phone's hardware might be capable of more than the standard allows.
key_points: 3GPP video spec limits playback to 15fps at 64 kbit/sec, yielding only 4.3 kbit per frame
Despite the low specs, 3GPP content is watchable, especially for simpler content like cartoons
Unrestricted MPEG-4 without frame rate or throughput limits would be a major improvement
The post questions why phones can't handle full MPEG-4 decoding natively
money_quotes: The specification mandates that movies run a maximum of 15 frames per second from a throughput of 64 kbit/sec, which leaves a paltry 4.3 kbit per frame.
It pales in comparison, though, with the unrestricted variety of MPEG-4 that doesn't limit frame rates or throughput.
So why not do unrestricted MPEG-4 decoding right on the phone?
key_insight: Mobile video standards in 2003 artificially constrained what phone hardware could actually handle, and DHH saw an opportunity to push past those limits.
